name: Adding and Subtracting Like Fractions
whatIsIt: Like fractions have the SAME bottom number — the same size pieces. Adding them is just counting pieces: 2/8 + 3/8 = 5/8, because 2 pieces plus 3 pieces of the same size make 5 pieces. The piece SIZE (the bottom) never changes — only the count (the top) does.
whyNeeded: Once pieces match, fractions behave exactly like counting toys! You ate 2 slices, your sister ate 3 slices of the same pizza — together how much of the pizza is gone? This is the doorway to ALL fraction arithmetic; later, equivalent fractions will make UNLIKE pieces match so this same trick works everywhere.
realLifeUses: Pizza night: 2/8 eaten by you + 3/8 by your sister = 5/8 of the pizza gone
Water bottle: drank 1/4 in the morning and 2/4 at lunch — 3/4 finished
Homework: finished 3/10 yesterday and 4/10 today — 7/10 done
Chapati sharing: 1/4 left from lunch plus 2/4 from dinner boxes

vocabulary.meaning: Fractions with the SAME bottom number — same-size pieces (2/8 and 3/8).
Different bottoms — different-size pieces (1/2 and 1/3). Not today's guests!
With like fractions, add the piece counts; the piece size stays.

story.title: Two Helpings at the Sankranti Feast
story.text: At the Sankranti feast, Ajji cuts a big sweet holige into 8 equal slices. Meena eats 2 slices before the kite-flying, then 3 more after. 'How much of the whole holige did I eat, Ajji?' she asks. Ajji smiles: 'The slices are all the same size — eighths. So just COUNT them: 2 slices and 3 slices make 5 slices. Nobody re-cut the holige, so it stays in eighths. You ate 5/8 of it!'
story.extractedProblem: 2/8 of a holige + 3/8 of the same holige — how much holige in total?
story.answerInStory: 5/8 — five same-size slices out of eight. The bottom stays because nobody re-cut the holige!

visual.caption: Same-size pieces: 2 shaded + 3 shaded = 5 shaded. Tops add, the bottom (piece size) stays 8.

teachingGallery.title: Adding like fractions — same bottom
teachingGallery.note: When the bottoms match, just add the tops. The bottom never changes.

standardMethod.summary: Same bottoms? Add (or subtract) the TOPS, keep the bottom, simplify if you can.
steps: Check the bottoms MATCH — like fractions only! (2/8 and 3/8 ✓)
Simplify if possible (4/8 would become 1/2; 5/8 is already simplest).

alternateMethods.name: Strip counting
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE the addition and never doubt the rule.
steps: Draw one strip of 8 equal parts.
Shade 2 parts one colour, then 3 more parts another colour.
Count ALL the shaded parts: that's the answer.
alternateMethods.example: 1/6 + 4/6 on a strip of 6: shade 1, then 4 → 5 shaded → 5/6.

alternateMethods.name: Number-line hops
alternateMethods.whenToUse: Connects fraction adding to the number line you already know.
steps: Start at 2/8 on a 0-to-1 line cut into 8 jumps.
alternateMethods.example: Subtracting hops backward: 7/8 − 3/8 → three hops back → 4/8 = 1/2.

workedExamples.problem: A water bottle scale shows eighths. You drank 3/8 before lunch and 2/8 after. What fraction of the bottle did you drink?
steps: Keep the bottom: 5/8 of the bottle.

workedExamples.problem: A chapati is in sixths. 5/6 was on the plate; Ravi ate 2/6. How much is left? (Subtraction works the same way!)

visual.caption: The chapati stays in 6 equal slices — 3 slices are left: 3/6.

trickPractice.intro: When bottoms match, the bottom stays and you simply add the top numbers.

hintLadder: The bottoms are the SAME, so keep the bottom.
Add the tops: 2 + 1 = 3.
The bottoms are the SAME, so keep the bottom.
Add the tops: 1 + 2 = 3.
The bottoms are the SAME, so keep the bottom.
Add the tops: 3 + 2 = 5.
The bottoms are the SAME, so keep the bottom.
Add the tops: 1 + 3 = 4.
The bottoms are the SAME, so keep the bottom.
Add the tops: 4 + 3 = 7.
The bottoms are the SAME, so keep the bottom.
Add the tops: 2 + 2 = 4.

commonMistakes.fix: The bottom is the PIECE SIZE, and nobody re-cut the pizza! Only the piece COUNT (top) changes. Add tops, keep the bottom: 5/8.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using this rule on different bottoms: 1/2 + 1/3 = 2/5.
commonMistakes.fix: Different bottoms = different-size pieces — you can't just count them together! First make the pieces match with equivalent fractions (that adventure comes next). Today's rule is for LIKE fractions only.

commonMistakes.mistake: Leaving 4/8 as the final answer when it has a simpler name.
commonMistakes.fix: Always ask: does my answer have a smaller name? 4/8 = 1/2 (divide both by 4). Simplest names make answers easiest to see.

easy.q: 2/8 of the pizza + 3/8 of the pizza = ? (write the fraction)
hintLadder: Are the bottoms the same? Then the pieces match!
Just count the pieces: 2 + 3 = ?
5 pieces of size one-eighth: write top over bottom.
Did anyone re-cut the strip? The pieces stay sixths.
Add only the tops: 1 + 4.
5 pieces, each one-sixth.

medium.q: You finished 3/10 of your homework yesterday and 4/10 today. What fraction is done? (write the fraction)
hintLadder: Same bottoms — tenths and tenths.
Keep the /10.

medium.q: Subtraction, same trick: 7/8 − 3/8 = ? (write the fraction; simplify!)
hintLadder: Bottoms match — subtract only the tops.
7 − 3 = 4, keep the /8.
Bonus: 4/8 has a simpler name (1/2) — either is correct here, write 4/8.

challenge.q: Ravi writes 1/2 + 1/3 = 2/5. Fraction Fox faints. Why is this NOT allowed?

options.label: Halves and thirds are different-size pieces — they can't be counted together yet
Nothing wrong — tops and bottoms both add

challenge.answer: Halves and thirds are different-size pieces — they can't be counted together yet
hintLadder: What was today's ONE condition before adding?
Do 1/2 and 1/3 have the same bottom?
Unlike pieces must be renamed to matching pieces first (equivalent fractions!).

challenge.q: A juice jug is marked in sixths. Anu pours out 2/6, then 1/6 more, then drinks 1/6 herself. What fraction of the jug is GONE in total?
hintLadder: Three like fractions leaving the jug: 2/6 + 1/6 + 1/6.
Add all the tops: 2 + 1 + 1.
Keep the bottom: sixths.
Bottoms match — count the pieces.
Keep the /7.

questions.q: When adding like fractions, the bottom number…

options.label: Stays the same — it is the piece size, and nobody re-cut the whole

questions.answer: Stays the same — it is the piece size, and nobody re-cut the whole
hintLadder: Remember the pizza — did the slice marks change?
What does the bottom number describe?
Piece size stays; only the count grows.
Same bottoms — subtract only the tops.
Keep the /10. (Its simple name is 1/2!)

questions.q: Which pair can you add DIRECTLY with today's rule?
hintLadder: Today's rule needs matching what?
Check the bottoms of each pair.
Only same-bottom (like) fractions count pieces directly.

teachBackPrompt: Retell the Pizza Party Puzzle in your own words: what silly thing did Ravi do with 2/8 + 3/8, and why does the bottom number refuse to change? Then show 1/6 + 4/6 on a drawn strip.

revisionCard.summary: LIKE fractions (same bottoms) = same-size pieces → just count them! Add/subtract the TOPS, keep the bottom: 2/8+3/8=5/8, 7/8−3/8=4/8=1/2. Never add bottoms (the pizza doesn't re-cut itself!). Unlike bottoms need renaming first.

gameMission.title: The Pizza Party Ledger
gameMission.brief: Guests keep eating slices and the Kitchen must track every pizza! Add and subtract the like fractions on Fox's ledger — and catch Ravi whenever he tries to 're-cut' a pizza by adding the bottoms.

realLifeProject: At the next meal, cut one roti into 4 equal parts (or a dosa into 8). Track out loud who takes what: '1/4 gone... now 2/4 gone...' Write the full addition story of your family finishing the roti, fraction by fraction.

rememberIt.hook: Same bottom? Add the tops, keep the bottom
rememberIt.unpack: 2/7 + 3/7 = 5/7. The bottom (piece size) stays the same; you are just counting more pieces. Different bottoms -> make them the same first.

studentQuestions.q: Why not add the bottoms too?
studentQuestions.a: The bottom tells the PIECE SIZE, which does not change. 2 sevenths + 3 sevenths = 5 sevenths, not 5 fourteenths.

studentQuestions.q: What if the bottoms are different?
studentQuestions.a: Make them the same first using equivalent fractions, THEN add the tops.
